title: Hallucination Reduction
lesson:
A hallucination is when a model states something false with total confidence — a fake court case, a made-up API function, a wrong date delivered in the same smooth voice as a true fact. The model is not lying. It literally cannot tell the difference between "true" and "sounds-like-the-truth." Hallucination reduction is the bundle of tricks we use to make confident nonsense rarer.

Why models hallucinate at all

Remember what a language model actually does: it predicts the next token (a word-piece) that fits the pattern of everything before it. It was trained to be fluent, not honest.

Truth and plausibility usually overlap, so most of the time it is right by accident of good patterns. But when it does not know, it does not stop — it keeps generating the most likely-looking continuation. Fluency never switches off.

Think of a confident dinner-party guest who never says "I don't know." Ask them anything and they produce a smooth, grammatical answer because staying silent feels worse to them than being wrong. That is your model. A second analogy: it is like autocomplete on your phone that, instead of stopping at the edge of what it knows, cheerfully invents a plausible rest of the sentence.

There is also a sneaky training cause. When we score answers in RLHF (training a model from human ratings of its replies), a confident wrong guess often scores better than an honest "I'm not sure," because graders skim and reward fluency. So we accidentally reward bluffing. A third cause is staleness: every model has a knowledge cutoff, a date after which it has seen nothing, so questions about recent events have no real answer to recall — only a confident guess to invent.

The four levers you can pull

Reduction happens at four stages, and good products stack several of them.

  • Grounding (biggest lever): give the model the real source text at answer time so it copies facts instead of recalling them. This is RAG — retrieval-augmented generation. If the answer is sitting in the prompt, there is far less to invent.
  • Data: clean training data and include examples where the right answer is "I don't know" or "the document doesn't say." See SFT Datasets and Data Curation. If you fine-tune on facts the model has never seen, you can actually teach it to hallucinate.
  • Preference training: use Preference Datasets and DPO to reward calibrated honesty — picking "I'm not certain, but..." over a clean-sounding fabrication.
  • Inference-time guardrails: lower the temperature (a dial controlling randomness; lower means safer, more predictable picks), ask for citations, and verify the answer with a second pass.

Grounding in one snippet

The single highest-leverage move is to feed retrieved text and forbid guessing:

Answer ONLY using the context below.
If the answer is not in the context, reply exactly: "Not in the provided documents."

Context:
{retrieved_chunks}

Question: {user_question}

That tiny instruction — give it permission to refuse — kills a surprising share of hallucinations, because you remove the pressure to produce an answer no matter what.

Catching them automatically

You can also let the model second-guess itself. A common pattern is self-consistency: ask the same question a few times, and if the answers disagree wildly, that is a strong smell of a hallucination.

answers = [ask(model, question) for _ in range(5)]
if len(set(normalize(a) for a in answers)) > 2:
    flag_for_review(question)  # the model is not sure

For higher stakes you add a verifier: a second call (or a real tool like a search API or database lookup) that checks each claim against a source before you show it. This costs more tokens and latency, so reserve it for answers where being wrong is expensive — medical, legal, financial.

The key insight across all of these is that you stop asking the model to be a perfect memory and start treating it as a careful reader: hand it the facts, let it decline, and check its work.

Won't fix hallucinationActually reduces it
"Be accurate, don't make things up" in the prompt aloneGrounding the answer in retrieved source text (RAG)
A bigger model (helps a bit, never solves it)Permission to say "I don't know" + a verification step
High temperature for "creativity"Low temperature + citations on factual tasks
Fine-tuning on lots of new, unseen factsFine-tuning that teaches calibrated refusal

⚠️ You cannot reach zero. Every shipping system needs a plan for the hallucinations that slip through: show sources, flag low-confidence answers, and keep a human in the loop where the cost of being wrong is high.
mentalModel: A confident dinner-party guest who would rather give a smooth, grammatical wrong answer than ever admit "I don't know." Hallucination reduction is mostly about (a) handing them the facts to read from, and (b) making it socially OK for them to say "not sure."
takeaways: Models hallucinate because they're trained for fluency, not truth.
Grounding answers in retrieved source text (RAG) is the biggest lever.
Give the model permission to refuse, then verify high-stakes claims.
mistakes: Thinking the prompt "don't hallucinate" is enough. Telling a fluent guesser to stop guessing barely moves the needle. You need grounding (real source text in the prompt) and an explicit escape hatch to refuse, not just a polite request.

Assuming a bigger model fixes it. Larger models hallucinate less often but often more convincingly — the prose gets smoother while staying wrong. Size is a small lever; grounding and verification are the big ones.

Fine-tuning on facts the model never learned in pretraining. This is the classic own-goal: you teach the confident style of asserting facts, so it generalises to confidently asserting facts it doesn't actually hold. Teach behaviour and refusal, get fresh facts from RAG.

Cranking temperature up on factual tasks. High randomness is great for brainstorming and terrible for facts. For anything that must be correct, use a low temperature so the model picks its safest, most-supported tokens.

Trusting confidence words as evidence. "Definitely," "according to the official docs," and even a fake citation are just more fluent text the model generated. Verify against a real source; never treat the model's tone as proof.

Forgetting to measure it. Without an eval set of tricky questions (including ones with no answer), you can't tell if a change helped. See Human Evals — track a hallucination rate before and after every change.

diagram.caption: Without a source the model recalls and may fabricate; grounding it in retrieved text lets it cite facts or honestly refuse.

exercise:
Take any chat model and ask it five questions you know it cannot truly answer — a made-up person ("Summarise the 2019 paper by Dr. Elara Voss on quantum lettuce"), a non-existent API method, an event after its knowledge cutoff. Record how often it confidently invents an answer versus admits uncertainty. Then add one line to your prompt: "If you are not sure, say so and explain why." Re-run the same five and compare.

Stretch goal: Build a tiny RAG setup — drop 3 short text documents into the prompt as Context:, instruct the model to answer only from them and reply "Not in the documents" otherwise. Ask one question that is answered by the docs and one that isn't, and confirm it correctly refuses the second.
solution: Hint: On the first pass, most models will happily fabricate a confident summary of the fake paper and invent a plausible-looking API method — fluency without truth. Adding the "say so if unsure" line usually flips several of those into honest hedges, because you've given it permission to refuse. In the stretch goal, the grounded prompt should answer the in-document question accurately and reply "Not in the documents" for the other one. If it still invents an answer for the missing fact, your instruction wasn't strict enough: make the refusal wording exact and explicit ("reply exactly: ...") and lower the temperature toward 0. The lesson: behaviour and grounding beat scolding.
summary: Hallucinations happen because models are trained to be fluent, not truthful, and never learned to stop when they don't know. You can't eliminate them, but you reduce them by stacking levers: ground answers in retrieved source text (RAG), train on data that includes honest "I don't know," prefer calibrated refusal over bluffing, lower temperature, and verify high-stakes claims against a real source. Always measure the rate and keep a human in the loop where being wrong is costly.

keyTerms.def: When a model states false information confidently and fluently, with no signal that it is wrong. It is plausible-sounding text, not a deliberate lie.
Giving the model the real source text at answer time so it copies facts from the prompt instead of recalling (and possibly inventing) them.
Fetching relevant documents and putting them in the prompt before the model answers, so the facts it needs are right in front of it.
A dial for randomness in token selection. Low temperature picks the safest, most likely words (better for facts); high temperature is more varied (better for creativity).
How well a model's expressed confidence matches its actual chance of being right. A well-calibrated model hedges when it is genuinely unsure.
Asking the same question several times; if the answers disagree, that disagreement is a strong hint the model is uncertain and may be hallucinating.
A second step — another model call or a real tool like search or a database — that checks each claim against a source before the answer is shown.
The date after which a model has no training data. Questions about events past this date are a common trigger for confident hallucination.
The model declining to answer ("I don't know" / "not in the documents"). Teaching and rewarding this is a core hallucination-reduction technique.

quiz.q: Why do language models hallucinate in the first place?
options: They are trained to predict fluent next tokens, not to verify truth, and never stop when unsure
They deliberately lie to seem more helpful
Their training data contains only false facts
They run out of memory mid-answer and improvise
quiz.explain: A model optimises for plausible-sounding continuations, so when it doesn't know it keeps generating fluent text instead of stopping. It has no built-in sense of true versus false.

quiz.q: Which single change typically reduces hallucinations the most for a factual Q&A product?
options: Adding 'please be accurate and don't make things up' to the prompt
Grounding the answer in retrieved source text (RAG) and allowing the model to refuse
Raising the temperature so the model explores more answers
Swapping to the largest available model
quiz.explain: Grounding puts the real facts in the prompt so there's little to invent, and a refusal escape hatch removes the pressure to guess. A bigger model and a scolding prompt help only marginally.

quiz.q: You fine-tune a model on 500 fresh facts it never saw during pretraining. What's the likely risk?
options: It will permanently memorise every fact with no downside
It learns the confident style of asserting facts and hallucinates more on unseen questions
It will refuse to answer anything afterwards
Its temperature setting gets locked to zero
quiz.explain: Teaching a model to confidently state facts it hasn't truly internalised reinforces the bluffing behaviour, which generalises to confidently asserting things it doesn't know. Use RAG for fresh facts instead.

quiz.q: A medical chatbot must minimise wrong answers even at higher cost. Best approach?
options: Trust the model's confident tone since it sounds authoritative
Increase temperature to get more creative phrasing
Ground in trusted sources, then run a verifier step that checks each claim before showing it
Tell the model in the prompt to 'only say true things'
quiz.explain: High-stakes domains justify the extra tokens and latency of grounding plus a verification pass against real sources. Confident tone is not evidence, and higher temperature only adds risk.
